facets.Problem Formalization: Solve ∇²u(x,y) = f(x,y) on a rectangular domain Ω = [x_min, x_max] × [y_min, y_max] with Dirichlet boundary condition u|∂Ω = g(x,y). The source term f is known analytically or pointwise, and g is prescribed on the boundary. Missing specifications: exact domain extents, functional form of f and g, and any regularity assumptions on the data. These must be provided to instantiate the workflow; their absence prevents determination of solution smoothness and appropriate sampling density.
facets.Physics Constraints: Primary constraint: PDE residual r(x,y) = ∂²u/∂x² + ∂²u/∂y² − f(x,y) = 0 at all interior points. Secondary constraint: Dirichlet boundary condition u(x,y) = g(x,y) on ∂Ω. Since the Poisson equation is elliptic, the solution inherits regularity from f and g; if both are smooth, u is smooth, and no additional conservation or compatibility constraints are needed beyond well-posedness (the Fredholm alternative is automatically satisfied for Poisson with Dirichlet BCs).
facets.Model Choice: Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) using a fully-connected MLP. Justification: (1) Elliptic PDEs yield smooth solutions well-approximated by MLPs of moderate depth (4–6 layers, 64–128 neurons per layer) with tanh or sin activations; (2) No time-dependence or advection, so no special architectures (e.g., causal weighting, Fourier features) are strictly needed; (3) Hard-constraint option: encode Dirichlet BCs by constructing û = g + d(x,y)·N(x,y) where d(x,y) is a smooth distance-like function vanishing on ∂Ω, eliminating BC loss and improving accuracy. Soft-constraint alternative: penalize BC violations in the loss. Hard constraints are preferred for Dirichlet problems to avoid boundary penalty tuning.
facets.Training Strategy: Loss function: L = λ_pde · (1/N_r) Σ|r(x_i,y_i)|² + λ_bc · (1/N_b) Σ|u(x_j,y_j) − g(x_j,y_j)|² (soft constraint) or L = (1/N_r) Σ|r(x_i,y_i)|² (hard constraint). Sampling: N_r = 10,000–50,000 interior collocation points via Latin Hypercube Sampling on Ω; N_b = 400–1,000 boundary points uniformly distributed on ∂Ω. Residual points can be resampled periodically to reduce overfitting. Optimization: Adam (lr=1e-3) for initial convergence (~10k–50k iterations), followed by L-BFGS for fine-tuning. Automatic differentiation computes second derivatives for the Laplacian. Weighting: if using soft constraints, start with λ_bc ≫ λ_pde (e.g., 100:1) and optionally use adaptive weighting (e.g., GradNorm or NeurIPS-style residual-based attention).
facets.Validation Failure Risks: (1) Insufficient collocation density leading to unresolved features in f or u—mitigate by residual-based adaptive refinement; (2) Soft BC enforcement with poor weight tuning causing boundary leakage—mitigate by hard constraints or progressive weight increase; (3) Spectral bias causing slow convergence for high-frequency components in f or g—mitigate by Fourier feature embeddings if needed; (4) Missing exact specifications of f, g, and domain dimensions—without these, no ground-truth validation is possible; (5) Overfitting to collocation noise if f is provided from noisy data rather than analytically—use regularization or denoising. Validation metrics: L2 relative error against analytical solution (if available), PDE residual norm on a held-out test grid, and boundary condition violation norm.
